question: Design a `NotificationSystem` class. Overload its constructor to accept different notification types (e.g., `enum NotificationType { SMS, EMAIL, PUSH }`). Also, overload a `send` method to handle sending messages of different content types (e.g., `std::string`, `int`).

test_cases.input: Execution of the main function, demonstrating various `NotificationSystem` operations with readability and efficiency issues.
test_cases.expected:
Starting notification tests.

Notifier created with type: SMS
Sending a message: "Test SMS message number one." via SMS. Content length is 28.
--- End of string send operation ---
Sending an integer: 101 via SMS. Numeric code: 101.
--- End of int send operation ---

Notifier created with type: EMAIL
Sending a message: "Hello, this is an important email to read right now!" via EMAIL. Subject preview: Hello, this is an importa...
--- End of string send operation ---
Sending an integer: 202 via EMAIL. Error code: 202.
--- End of int send operation ---

Notifier created with type: PUSH
Sending a message: "New update available for your application." via PUSH. Alert text: New update available for your application..
--- End of string send operation ---
Sending an integer: 303 via PUSH. Status update: 303.
--- End of int send operation ---

Notifier created with int type value: 5
Sending a message: "This message should not be sent effectively." via Unknown notification type. Message not sent.
--- End of string send operation ---
Sending an integer: 500 via Unknown notification type. Integer not sent.
--- End of int send operation ---

All notification tests completed.

errors: The `myType` member variable name is not descriptive, hindering code understanding.
The `send` methods use long `if-else if` chains to handle `NotificationType`, which is less readable and potentially less efficient than a `switch` statement, especially as more types are added.

Excessive use of `std::endl` instead of `'
'` causes frequent buffer flushes, leading to performance overhead, especially in high-volume logging scenarios.

String concatenation using `+` and `std::to_string` for output (`"Content length is " + std::to_string(msg.length())`) creates unnecessary temporary string objects, which is less efficient than direct stream insertion.
Repetitive output patterns and string creations (e.g., `std::string prefix = "Sending a message: ";`) reduce code readability and introduce minor inefficiencies.
